steps: Bring water to a rolling boil in a heavy-bottomed pan. Add salt.
Reduce heat to medium and gradually add ragi flour while stirring continuously to prevent lumps.
Mix vigorously with a wooden spoon until a thick, smooth dough forms and leaves the sides of the pan.
Cover and cook on low heat for 3–4 minutes until fully cooked through.
Wet your hands, shape portions into smooth balls while still hot.
Serve immediately with sambar, saaru (rasam), or chutney.

ingredients: ¼ cup urad dal (split black gram), soaked 4 hours
steps: Grind soaked urad dal to a smooth batter using a little water.
Mix ragi flour, rice flour, and urad dal batter together. Add enough water to make a thin, pourable consistency.
Stir in cumin seeds, green chillies, ginger, and curry leaves. Add salt.
Ferment the batter for 6–8 hours or overnight at room temperature.
Heat a cast-iron or non-stick tawa. Pour a ladleful of batter and spread in a thin circle.
Drizzle a few drops of oil on the edges. Cook until the base is golden and crisp.
Fold and serve hot with coconut chutney or sambar.
Place jowar flour in a bowl, add salt and mix.
Gradually add warm water and knead into a soft, pliable dough. Jowar dough is sticky — keep hands damp.
Pinch a lemon-sized ball and pat it into a round roti directly on a wet cotton cloth or your palm.
Transfer to a hot tawa (griddle) and cook on medium heat until small brown spots appear.
Flip and cook the other side. Press gently with a cloth to cook evenly.
Remove when both sides are done. Serve hot with ennegayi or chutney.
Heat oil in a pan. Add mustard seeds and let them splutter.
Add urad dal and fry until golden. Add dried red chillies and curry leaves.
Add green chillies and sauté for 30 seconds.
Add grated coconut and stir-fry on low heat for 2 minutes until fragrant.
Add cooked rice and salt. Mix gently so the rice doesn't break.
Cook for 2 more minutes until well combined. Garnish with coriander and serve.

ingredients: Fresh coriander and grated coconut to garnish
steps: Dry-roast semolina in a pan on medium heat until lightly golden and fragrant. Set aside.
Heat oil in the same pan. Add mustard seeds; let them splutter.
Add chana dal, urad dal, and fry until golden brown.
Add onion, green chillies, ginger, and curry leaves. Sauté until onion is translucent.
Pour in 2 cups of water and bring to a boil. Add salt.
Reduce heat and slowly add roasted semolina while stirring continuously to prevent lumps.
Cover and cook on low heat for 3–4 minutes, stirring once or twice.
Fluff with a spoon, squeeze lemon if desired, garnish and serve hot.

ingredients: For sambar: ½ cup toor dal, 1 tomato, 1 drumstick, 1 onion, tamarind water, sambar powder, mustard seeds, curry leaves, oil
steps: Grind urad dal to a smooth, fluffy batter. Grind idli rice to a coarse batter.
Mix both batters with salt. Ferment overnight (8–10 hours) until doubled in volume.
Grease idli moulds. Pour batter into moulds filling ¾ full.
Steam in an idli steamer for 10–12 minutes. Check doneness with a wet finger — it should come out clean.
For sambar: pressure-cook toor dal with tomato until soft. Sauté onion, add sambar powder, tamarind water, cooked dal, and vegetables. Simmer 15 minutes.
Temper sambar with mustard seeds and curry leaves in oil. Serve hot with idli.
Mix rice flour with onion, green chillies, coconut, curry leaves, coriander, and salt.
Gradually add hot water and knead into a soft, smooth dough.
Divide into lemon-sized balls. Wet a banana leaf or plastic sheet.
Place a ball on the surface and pat into a thin, even circle using damp fingers.
Heat a tawa on medium flame. Slide the roti onto the tawa.
Cover and cook for 2 minutes. Drizzle oil on edges, flip and cook uncovered until golden spots appear.
Serve hot with butter, coconut chutney, or sambar.

ingredients: 1 cup mixed vegetables (carrot, beans, peas, potato)
steps: Wash rice and dal together. Pressure-cook with 4 cups water for 4 whistles until very soft.
Meanwhile, cook mixed vegetables until tender.
Mix tamarind paste, bisibelebath powder, jaggery, and salt with ½ cup water.
Combine cooked rice-dal, vegetables, and tamarind mixture in a heavy pan.
Simmer on low heat, stirring often, adding water if too thick. Cook for 10 minutes.
For tempering: heat ghee, splutter mustard seeds, add curry leaves, red chillies, and cashews.
Pour tempering over bisibelebath, mix and serve immediately — it thickens as it cools.

ingredients: For Ennegayi: 4 small brinjals (slit), 3 tbsp oil, 1 onion, 1 tomato, ennegayi masala (peanut, sesame, dry coconut, spices), tamarind, jaggery, salt
steps: Make jowar rotti: knead jowar flour with warm water into pliable dough. Pat each ball into a thin round on a damp cloth.
Cook rotti on a hot griddle, pressing with a cloth until brown spots appear on both sides.
For ennegayi: dry-roast peanuts, sesame, dry coconut, and whole spices. Grind to a thick paste with garlic and tamarind.
Slit brinjals lengthwise keeping stem intact; stuff each with the masala paste.
Heat oil, fry onion until golden. Add stuffed brinjals and sauté gently.
Add tomato, tamarind water, jaggery, and remaining masala. Cook covered on low flame for 15–20 minutes.
Serve hot stuffed brinjal curry alongside jolada rotti.
Grind soaked rice with coconut to an absolutely smooth, fine batter.
Add water gradually to make a very thin, watery batter (thinner than buttermilk).
Add salt and mix well. The batter should pour freely off the ladle.
Heat a non-stick tawa on medium heat. Lightly grease with a few drops of oil.
Pour a ladleful of batter and quickly swirl to coat the pan thinly. Don't spread — let it flow.
Cover and cook for 1–2 minutes. The dosa is ready when the edges lift and the surface turns opaque.
Do not flip. Gently fold into half or thirds. Serve immediately with coconut chutney or curry.

ingredients: ½ cup roasted peanuts, coarsely crushed
Salt and lemon juice to taste
steps: Soak sabudana in water overnight; they should be soft and non-sticky the next morning. Drain thoroughly.
Mix drained sabudana with crushed peanuts, salt, and a pinch of sugar.
Heat oil in a pan. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter. Add curry leaves and green chillies.
Add boiled potato cubes and sauté for 2 minutes.
Add the sabudana-peanut mixture. Toss gently on medium-low heat for 5–7 minutes.
Each pearl should become translucent when fully cooked.
Squeeze lemon juice, garnish with coriander, and serve warm.

ingredients: ½ cup toor dal or masoor dal
2 cups palak (spinach), washed and roughly chopped
steps: Pressure-cook dal with turmeric, tomato, and 2 cups water for 3–4 whistles until fully soft.
Mash dal lightly and set aside.
Heat oil in a pan. Add cumin seeds, then onion and garlic. Sauté until golden.
Add coriander powder and cook 1 minute. Add spinach and sauté until wilted (3–4 minutes).
Combine spinach mixture with mashed dal. Add garam masala and salt.
Simmer together on low heat for 5 minutes until flavours meld.
Serve hot with rice or roti. Best with a dash of lemon juice.
Dry-roast moong dal in a pan until lightly golden and fragrant.
Pressure-cook roasted dal with 1½ cups water for 2 whistles until soft but not mushy.
In a thick-bottomed pan, dissolve jaggery in ¼ cup water on low heat. Strain to remove impurities.
Add cooked dal to jaggery syrup and stir well. Cook on medium heat for 5 minutes.
Add thin coconut milk and simmer for 5 more minutes, stirring continuously.
Add thick coconut milk and cardamom. Cook on very low heat for 3 minutes — do not boil.
Fry cashews and raisins in ghee until golden. Pour over payasam and serve warm.

ingredients: For tempering: 1 tsp mustard seeds, 1 tsp urad dal, 1 sprig curry leaves, 1 tbsp oil
steps: Dry-roast peanuts until skins begin to crack. Cool and rub off the skins.
In a dry pan, lightly toast red chillies and garlic.
Grind peanuts, red chillies, garlic, tamarind, and salt to a coarse, slightly moist chutney powder.
Heat oil. Add mustard seeds and let them pop. Add urad dal and curry leaves — fry 30 seconds.
Add cooked rice and the peanut chutney. Mix well over low heat for 2–3 minutes.
Taste and adjust salt. Serve warm with papad or yogurt.
Pressure-cook rice and dal together with 4 cups water and turmeric for 4 whistles.
Pressure-cook drumstick and potato in a separate small vessel with water.
In a large pan, heat oil. Add mustard seeds, curry leaves, and onion. Sauté until golden.
Add tomato and sambar powder; cook until oil separates.
Add tamarind paste and ½ cup water. Simmer 2 minutes.
Add cooked dal-rice and vegetables. Mix well and simmer on low heat 10 minutes.
Adjust consistency with water. Serve hot with ghee, papad, and pickle.
Remove moringa leaves from stems. Wash thoroughly.
Heat oil in a pan. Add cumin seeds; let splutter.
Add onion and sauté until light golden. Add tomatoes and cook until pulpy.
Add turmeric, coriander-cumin powder, and red chilli powder. Cook the masala for 2 minutes.
Add drumstick leaves and stir well. Cover and cook on medium heat 5 minutes.
Add cooked toor dal, salt, and ½ cup water. Simmer uncovered 5 more minutes.
Serve hot with rice or ragi mudde.
Pressure-cook soaked horsegram with 3 cups water for 5–6 whistles. Mash slightly.
Strain through a sieve, reserving the liquid (saaru) and the cooked dal separately.
Heat oil in a pan. Splutter mustard seeds, add curry leaves and red chillies.
Add tomato and cook until soft. Add tamarind paste, saaru powder, turmeric, jaggery, and salt.
Pour in the horsegram liquid. Bring to a boil and simmer 10 minutes.
Adjust salt and tanginess. The saaru should be thin, tangy, and aromatic.
Serve hot with rice or ragi mudde. The cooked horsegram can be served as a side palya.
Mix wheat flour and rice flour together.
Add onion, cumin seeds, green chilli, and salt.
Add water gradually to make a smooth, thin batter (consistency of dosa batter).
Heat a non-stick tawa on medium heat. Lightly oil it.
Pour a ladleful of batter and spread into a thin circle.
Drizzle oil on edges. Cook until bottom turns golden-brown and edges crisp up.
Flip and cook the other side briefly. Serve with chutney or sambar.
Heat ghee in a heavy pan. Fry cashews and raisins until golden; remove and set aside.
In the same ghee, roast semolina on medium heat, stirring constantly, until golden and fragrant (5–6 min).
Reduce heat to low. Carefully pour in warm milk while stirring to prevent lumps.
Add mashed banana and mix thoroughly. Cook 3–4 minutes until semolina absorbs the milk.
Add sugar/jaggery and cardamom. Stir and cook until the mixture comes together and leaves the sides of the pan.
Add more ghee if desired. Garnish with fried cashews, raisins, and saffron.
Serve warm as a breakfast or dessert.

ingredients: 1 medium potato, diced small and fried or boiled
Lemon juice, fresh coriander, sev, and grated coconut for garnish
steps: Rinse poha in a colander under running water until soft. Drain well — it should be moist but not soggy.
Mix turmeric and salt into the soaked poha. Set aside.
Heat oil in a wide pan. Add mustard seeds; let them splutter.
Add curry leaves, green chillies, and onion. Sauté until onion is translucent.
Add fried or boiled potato and mix well.
Add the seasoned poha and toss gently on medium-low heat for 3–4 minutes.
Squeeze lemon juice, garnish with coriander, sev, and grated coconut. Serve immediately.
Rub oil on your hands before handling raw jackfruit to prevent stickiness.
Remove the outer skin and inner seeds of the jackfruit. Cut into bite-sized pieces.
Pressure-cook jackfruit with ½ tsp turmeric and salt in water for 2 whistles.
Dry-roast coriander seeds, cumin seeds, and red chillies. Grind with coconut and onion to a smooth paste.
Heat oil, add the ground paste, and sauté for 3–4 minutes.
Add cooked jackfruit and mix to coat with the masala. Add water to desired consistency.
Simmer covered for 10 minutes. Serve with rice or ragi mudde.

ingredients: 2 cups fresh avarekalu (field beans/hyacinth beans), shelled
2 tbsp avarekalu saaru masala or saaru powder
steps: Pressure-cook avarekalu with 2 cups water for 2 whistles.
Heat oil in a pan. Add mustard seeds; let splutter. Add curry leaves and onion.
Sauté onion until golden. Add tomatoes and cook until soft.
Add saaru masala powder, tamarind, jaggery, and salt. Stir well.
Add cooked beans with their liquid. Bring to a boil.
Simmer for 10–15 minutes until the curry thickens and flavours meld.
Serve piping hot with jolada rotti or rice.

ingredients: Pomegranate seeds and coriander to garnish
steps: Lightly mash the cooked rice while it is still warm.
Mix in milk first to loosen the rice, then fold in yogurt gently.
Add salt and grated ginger. The consistency should be creamy — add more milk if too thick.
Heat oil in a small pan. Splutter mustard seeds, fry urad dal until golden.
Add red chilli, curry leaves, and green chilli. Fry for 30 seconds.
Pour the hot tempering over the curd rice and mix gently.
Garnish with pomegranate seeds and coriander. Serve at room temperature or chilled.
Boil eggs for 10 minutes. Peel and make 2–3 shallow cuts on each egg. Lightly fry in oil for 2 minutes; set aside.
Heat oil in a pan. Add cumin seeds; let splutter. Add onions and sauté until deep golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook 2 minutes.
Add tomato puree, coriander powder, cumin powder, turmeric, and red chilli powder. Cook masala until oil separates.
Add 1 cup water and bring to a simmer.
Add boiled eggs gently. Simmer 5 minutes, spooning gravy over them.
Add garam masala and salt. Serve over steamed rice, garnished with coriander.
Boil eggs in water for 10 minutes. Transfer to cold water, peel, and halve. Sprinkle with salt.
For ragi mudde: bring water and salt to a rolling boil in a heavy pan.
Add ragi flour while stirring continuously. Mix vigorously to eliminate lumps.
Cover and cook on low heat 3–4 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the dough is cooked through.
With wet hands, shape into smooth balls.
Serve ragi mudde warm with boiled eggs and a bowl of hot saaru or sambar.

ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Crack eggs into a bowl. Add onion, tomato, green chilli, coriander, turmeric, salt, and pepper.
Beat well until fully combined and slightly frothy.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan over medium heat.
Pour the egg mixture. Swirl to spread evenly. Reduce heat to medium-low.
Cook until the bottom sets and edges begin to leave the pan (2–3 minutes).
Fold the omelette in half. Cook 1 more minute until fully set inside.
Toast bread slices. Serve the hot masala omelette with toast.

ingredients: 500g fresh fish (pomfret, mackerel, or sardines), cleaned and cut
steps: Grind coconut, red chillies, coriander seeds, cumin seeds, and turmeric to a very smooth paste with minimal water.
Add tamarind paste to the ground masala and mix.
Heat oil in a clay pot or heavy pan. Fry sliced onion until golden.
Add the masala paste and sauté for 3–4 minutes until fragrant.
Add 1½ cups water and bring to a simmer. Add salt.
Gently add fish pieces. Do not stir — shake the pan to move pieces.
Cook covered on low-medium heat for 10–12 minutes until fish is cooked through.
Rest for 10 minutes before serving. Serve with steamed parboiled rice.

ingredients: 2 tbsp saaru masala or chicken masala powder
steps: Heat oil in a pressure cooker. Add onions and fry until deep golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook 2 minutes until raw smell disappears.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, and masala powder. Cook until oil separates.
Add chicken pieces, mix well to coat with masala.
Add 1½ cups water and pressure-cook for 4–5 whistles.
Open when pressure releases. Simmer uncovered 5 minutes to thicken.
Make jolada rotti: knead jowar flour with warm water, pat into rotis, and cook on griddle.
Serve hot chicken saaru with freshly made jolada rotti and coriander garnish.
Dry-roast chillies, coriander, cumin, and peppercorns until fragrant. Grind to smooth paste with garlic, tamarind, and a little water.
Marinate cleaned prawns in half the masala paste with turmeric and salt for 30 minutes.
Heat ghee in a wide pan over medium heat. Add remaining masala and fry for 5 minutes until dark and fragrant.
Add marinated prawns and toss to coat. Cook on high heat 3–4 minutes, stirring often.
Add sugar and stir. Reduce heat and cook until masala coats prawns and ghee separates.
The roast should be dry and each prawn well-coated. Do not add water.
Serve on freshly made neer dosa with sliced onion and lemon.

ingredients: Acidulated water (water with lemon juice, for soaking)
steps: Prepare banana flower: remove outer purple bracts layer by layer. Collect the tender white florets. Remove the thin inner sheath and long stamen from each floret.
Soak cleaned florets in acidulated water for 20 minutes to prevent browning.
Pressure-cook florets with turmeric and salt for 2 whistles.
Dry-roast red chillies and coriander seeds. Grind coarsely with grated coconut.
Heat oil. Splutter mustard seeds, add curry leaves.
Add drained cooked florets and the ground coconut masala. Mix well.
Cook on medium heat 5 minutes, tossing gently. Serve with rice.
Dry-roast peanuts until skins start to crack. Cool slightly, then rub in your palms to remove skins.
Coarsely crush peanuts in a grinder (not too fine — some chunky pieces add texture).
In a pan, melt jaggery with 2 tbsp water on low heat until it forms a soft-ball stage syrup.
Quickly add crushed peanuts, cardamom, and ghee to the jaggery. Mix fast.
While the mixture is still warm and pliable, shape into small balls (laddus) using your palms.
Work quickly as the mixture sets on cooling. If it hardens too fast, briefly warm it.
Allow laddus to cool completely. Store in an airtight container up to 1 week.

ingredients: 1 cup fresh methi (fenugreek) leaves, finely chopped
steps: Wash and finely chop methi leaves. Sprinkle with a pinch of salt and set aside 5 minutes to reduce bitterness.
In a bowl, mix wheat flour, ajwain, turmeric, red chilli powder, garam masala, and salt.
Add methi leaves (slightly squeezed) and mix into the flour.
Gradually add warm water and knead into a smooth, medium-soft dough. Rest 15 minutes.
Divide into golf-ball-sized portions. Roll each into a 6–7 inch circle.
Cook on a hot griddle. Drizzle oil and cook until golden brown spots appear. Flip and cook the other side.
Serve hot with yogurt, pickle, and white butter.
Dry-roast sesame seeds, peanuts, chana dal, and red chillies separately until golden. Let cool.
Grind roasted ingredients to a smooth powder.
Heat oil in a pan. Add mustard seeds; let splutter. Add curry leaves.
Add tomatoes and cook on medium heat until very soft and pulpy (8–10 minutes).
Add tamarind paste, jaggery, salt, and the ground powder.
Simmer on low heat for 5–7 minutes until the gojju thickens and oil rises to the top.
Serve over steamed rice with a dollop of ghee.

ingredients: 500g colocasia (arvi/taro), peeled and sliced into rounds
steps: Pressure-cook sliced colocasia with turmeric and a little salt for 1 whistle. Drain and cool.
Mix red chilli powder, coriander powder, and salt. Lightly toss cooked slices in this spice mix.
Heat oil in a flat pan. Add mustard seeds; let splutter. Add curry leaves.
Place spiced colocasia slices in a single layer. Do not overcrowd.
Cook on medium heat without stirring for 4–5 minutes until a golden crust forms.
Flip carefully and cook the other side similarly.
Garnish with coriander. Serve as a side with rice and dal.

ingredients: ½ cup moong dal or toor dal
1 cup mixed vegetables (carrot, peas, beans, potato), chopped
steps: Wash rice and dal together. Soak for 20 minutes. Drain.
Heat ghee in a pressure cooker. Add cumin seeds; let splutter.
Add onion and sauté until translucent. Add tomato and cook 2 minutes.
Add turmeric and garam masala. Mix well.
Add soaked rice-dal, vegetables, salt, and 4 cups water.
Pressure-cook for 4–5 whistles until everything is very soft and porridge-like.
Stir in more ghee if desired. Serve hot with yogurt, pickle, and papad.
Lightly roast puffed rice in a dry pan for 2 minutes to crisp it up.
In a large bowl, combine onion, tomatoes, green chillies, and peanuts.
Add red chilli powder, chaat masala, and salt. Mix well.
Add puffed rice and toss vigorously to coat.
Squeeze lemon juice generously and mix once more.
Garnish with coriander. Serve immediately (the puffed rice turns soggy if left too long).
Steam or parboil sweet potato cubes until just tender. Set aside.
Heat oil in a pan. Add cumin seeds; let splutter.
Add onion and sauté until golden. Add tomato puree.
Add turmeric, coriander powder, and cook masala until oil separates.
Add sweet potato cubes and toss gently to coat.
Add ½ cup water, salt, and a small piece of jaggery. Cover and cook 8–10 minutes.
Sprinkle garam masala. Garnish with coriander. Serve with chapati or rice.

ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: To sprout moong: soak overnight, drain, wrap in damp cloth, and leave for 12–24 hours until tiny sprouts appear.
Rinse sprouted moong. You can serve raw or lightly steam for 3 minutes for digestibility.
In a large bowl, mix sprouted moong with onion, tomato, and cucumber.
Add green chilli, lemon juice, chaat masala, salt, and pepper.
Toss well. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Garnish with coriander. Serve fresh as a snack, breakfast, or side salad.
Dry-roast moong dal until golden and fragrant.
Wash rice and roasted moong dal together.
Pressure-cook rice and dal with 3½ cups water and salt for 4–5 whistles until very soft and mushy.
Heat ghee in a small pan. Fry cashews until golden. Add cumin seeds, black pepper, ginger, and curry leaves.
Fry the tempering for 1 minute until aromatic.
Pour the tempering over the cooked rice-dal. Mix well.
Adjust consistency with hot water (pongal should be porridge-like). Serve hot with sambar and coconut chutney.

ingredients: 1 cup toor dal or mixed lentils
1 cup mixed vegetables (drumstick, raw mango, or leafy greens)
steps: Pressure-cook dal with vegetables and turmeric for 3 whistles.
Dry-roast Byadgi chillies and coriander seeds until fragrant.
Grind roasted spices with grated coconut and tamarind to a smooth paste.
Combine cooked dal with the ground masala. Add salt and water to adjust consistency.
Simmer on medium heat for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Finish with a drizzle of raw coconut oil for authentic coastal flavour.
Serve over steamed rice.

ingredients: A pinch of dry ginger powder (optional)
steps: Mix ragi flour with 2 tbsp cold water or milk to form a lump-free paste.
Heat remaining milk or water in a saucepan on medium heat.
When warm (not boiling), add the ragi paste while stirring continuously.
Keep stirring on low-medium heat for 4–5 minutes until the mixture thickens slightly.
Add jaggery or sugar and stir until dissolved.
Add cardamom powder and dry ginger if using.
Serve warm. Excellent as a morning drink for school children.

ingredients: 2 cups rice shavige (thin rice noodles) or semiya
Fresh coconut and coriander for garnish
steps: Cook shavige/vermicelli in boiling salted water for 3–4 minutes until just tender. Drain and set aside.
Heat oil in a pan. Add mustard seeds; let splutter. Add chana dal and urad dal; fry golden.
Add curry leaves, green chillies, and onion. Sauté until onion is translucent.
Add turmeric and mix.
Add cooked shavige and gently toss to coat with the tempering.
Add salt. Cook on low heat 3 minutes.
Squeeze lemon, garnish with coconut and coriander. Serve hot.

ingredients: 2 cups green beans (French beans), cut into pieces
For chapati: 2 cups whole wheat flour, water, salt
steps: Boil beans until just tender but still bright green. Drain.
Heat oil in a pan. Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook 2 minutes.
Add tomatoes, coriander powder, and turmeric. Cook until masala is thick and oil separates.
Add beans and salt. Mix well. Cook covered on low heat 5 minutes.
Sprinkle garam masala. Stir and serve.
For chapati: knead soft dough, rest 15 minutes, roll into thin rounds, and cook on hot tawa with a little ghee.

ingredients: 2 cups ragi flour (for mudde — see Ragi Mudde recipe)
For saaru: ½ cup toor dal, 2 tomatoes, 1 tsp tamarind paste, 2 tsp saaru powder, ½ tsp turmeric, mustard seeds, curry leaves, 2 tbsp ghee, salt
steps: Make ragi mudde (see Ragi Mudde recipe): boil water, add ragi flour, cook vigorously into balls.
For saaru: pressure-cook toor dal with tomatoes and turmeric until soft. Mash.
Mix tamarind paste and saaru powder with 2 cups water.
Add tamarind mixture to cooked dal. Bring to a boil.
Simmer for 10 minutes. Adjust salt and tanginess.
Heat ghee. Splutter mustard seeds, add curry leaves and pour over saaru.
Serve ragi mudde in a bowl of hot saaru — the traditional way to eat it in Karnataka.

ingredients: 4 large carrots, peeled and grated (about 3 cups grated)
½ cup condensed milk or ¾ cup sugar
steps: Heat 1 tbsp ghee in a heavy pan. Add grated carrots and sauté for 5 minutes on medium heat.
Add milk and cook on medium heat, stirring frequently, until milk is absorbed (15–20 min).
Add sugar or condensed milk. Stir continuously on medium-low heat.
When moisture reduces and halwa begins to leave sides of pan, add remaining ghee.
Add cardamom and saffron. Mix well. Cook 5 more minutes.
In a separate small pan, fry cashews and raisins in ghee until golden.
Garnish halwa with fried cashews and raisins. Serve warm or at room temperature.

ingredients: Fresh coriander and mint for garnish
steps: Heat oil in a pressure cooker. Fry onions until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook 2 minutes.
Add tomatoes, turmeric, and saaru masala powder. Cook until tomatoes break down.
Add chicken pieces and mix to coat with masala.
Add 3 cups water and pressure-cook for 5–6 whistles until chicken is very tender.
Open cooker when pressure releases. Taste and adjust salt.
Simmer uncovered 5 minutes if you want a more concentrated broth.
Garnish with coriander and mint. Serve hot as soup or with rice.

ingredients: 1 cup fresh dill leaves (sabsige soppu), roughly chopped
steps: Pressure-cook toor dal with turmeric and tomato for 3–4 whistles until very soft.
Heat oil in a pan. Add cumin seeds; let splutter. Add onion and sauté until translucent.
Add dill leaves and stir-fry on medium heat for 3–4 minutes.
Add red chilli powder and salt; cook 1 minute.
Add cooked dal and mix well. Stir in ½ cup water if too thick.
Simmer together 5 minutes. The dill should remain bright and fragrant.
Serve hot with rice. Best drizzled with a little ghee.
Grind soaked rice, urad dal, coconut, red chillies, tamarind, jaggery, and salt to a thick, smooth batter.
Wipe colocasia leaves dry. Place a large leaf vein-side up. Spread a thin layer of batter evenly.
Place another leaf on top (vein-side down) and spread more batter. Repeat 3–4 leaves.
Fold the sides of the stack inward and roll tightly into a log shape.
Steam the rolls for 40–45 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean.
Cool completely before slicing into rounds.
Serve as-is, or shallow-fry slices in a little oil until golden and crispy.

ingredients: Salt, water, and ghee for chapati
For chana masala: 1 cup dried chickpeas (soaked overnight), 2 onions, 2 tomatoes, 1 tbsp ginger-garlic paste, 2 tsp chana masala powder, ½ tsp cumin, ½ tsp turmeric, 2 tbsp oil
steps: Chapati: Knead wheat flour with salt and water into smooth dough. Rest 20 minutes.
Roll into thin rounds. Cook on hot tawa until brown spots appear. Puff over open flame briefly. Apply ghee.
Chana masala: Pressure-cook soaked chickpeas with salt for 5–6 whistles until tender.
Heat oil. Add cumin seeds, then onion; sauté until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste.
Add tomatoes and cook until pulpy. Add chana masala powder and turmeric.
Add cooked chickpeas with ½ cup cooking liquid. Simmer 15 minutes until thick gravy forms.
Serve hot chana masala with soft phulkas and sliced onion rings.

ingredients: Fresh coriander and grated coconut for garnish
steps: Cook rice and let it cool completely so grains are separate.
Heat oil in a wide pan. Add mustard seeds; let splutter. Add chana dal and urad dal; fry golden.
Add curry leaves and brinjal cubes. Cook on medium heat until brinjal is soft (8–10 min).
Add vangi bath masala, tamarind paste, jaggery, and salt. Stir well and cook 3 minutes.
Add cooked rice and gently toss to mix — avoid breaking grains.
Cook on low heat 5 minutes until flavours are absorbed.
Garnish with coriander and coconut. Serve with raita or papad.

ingredients: 1 tsp Eno fruit salt or baking soda
steps: Heat oil in a pan. Splutter mustard seeds, fry chana dal until golden. Add curry leaves and green chilli.
Add semolina and dry-roast with tempering for 3–4 minutes until lightly toasted.
Cool the roasted semolina. Mix in yogurt, water, carrot, and salt. Rest 10 minutes.
Just before steaming, add Eno fruit salt and stir gently. The batter will rise slightly.
Grease idli moulds with oil. Fill ¾ full.
Steam for 12–15 minutes until a skewer comes out clean.
Serve hot with coconut chutney and sambar. Best eaten fresh.
Mix turmeric and a pinch of salt into cooled rice. Set aside.
Heat oil in a pan. Add mustard seeds; let splutter.
Add chana dal, urad dal and fry until golden. Add dried red chillies.
Add curry leaves and green chillies; fry 30 seconds.
Add roasted peanuts and toss briefly.
Add the turmeric rice and mix gently to coat with tempering.
Remove from heat. Add lemon juice and salt. Toss well.
Serve at room temperature or slightly warm with papad and pickle.

ingredients: For ghee rice: 2 cups basmati rice, 2 tbsp ghee, 2 cardamom pods, 2 cloves, 1 bay leaf, salt
For toor dal: 1 cup toor dal, 2 tomatoes, 1 onion, 1 tsp cumin, ½ tsp turmeric, 1 tsp coriander powder, 1 tsp red chilli powder, 2 tbsp ghee, salt
steps: Ghee rice: Heat ghee in a pot. Add cardamom, cloves, bay leaf. Sauté 30 seconds.
Add washed basmati rice. Stir-fry 2 minutes until rice turns opaque.
Add hot water (1:1.5 ratio rice to water) and salt. Bring to boil, reduce heat to lowest, cover, cook 15 minutes.
Toor dal: Pressure-cook dal with tomatoes and turmeric for 4 whistles. Mash.
Heat ghee. Add cumin seeds; let splutter. Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add coriander powder, red chilli powder, and a little water. Cook masala 2 minutes.
Add cooked dal and simmer 10 minutes. Adjust salt and consistency.
Serve hot dal over ghee rice with papad and pickle.
